item.title: Overlapping pyramid vision Transformer enables fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification
item.dc:creator: Zequn Sun
Jiajie Ma
Chunning Meng
Lijun Zhao
Lianzhi Huo
Tao Li
Zhiqing Zhang
Shengjiang Chang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Ship detection from synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images has gained increasing attention recently due to the irreplaceable all-weather imaging capabilities of SAR. However, it remains unclear whether SAR imaging in combination with modern deep-learning is capable of detecting and classifying ships at the instance level, which is hampered by the lack of datasets with multi-class fine-grained annotations and the accompanying ship detection methods. To tackle these issues, we build a new multi-class fine-grained SAR ship dataset ISSC and an efficient multi-scale model for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification. The ISSC dataset has a number of 2764 SAR images and 8466 ship instances, with five categories. With this high-quality dataset that can reflect the diversity of ship scales, we further propose an efficient network for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification (the ISSC model), which uses overlapping pyramid vision Transformer as the model backbone to extract multi-scale feature maps that prioritize the continuous global information. Subsequently, based on convolution units, two neck structures are designed to refine the directional features representing various multi-class ships and amplify the local semantic information effect of the feature cascading process, respectively. As such, the ISSC model is a novel global-to-local and direction-aware ship detection network for SAR images. Experiments on the ISSC dataset and the single-class ship dataset HRSID and SSDD demonstrate the superior performance of the ISSC model, which has a lower parameter count and achieves substantial improvements, compared to state-of-the-art models. Overall, we have demonstrated the feasibility of combining SAR imaging with the modern deep learning approach for fine-grained ship classification. Published: 2026-05-20T19:53:14+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.824 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zequn Sun; Jiajie Ma; Chunning Meng; Lijun Zhao; Lianzhi Huo; Tao Li; Zhiqing Zhang; Shengjiang Chang

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3695474

Relevance: 0.824 (must_read)

Ship detection from synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images has gained increasing attention recently due to the irreplaceable all-weather imaging capabilities of SAR. However, it remains unclear whether SAR imaging in combination with modern deep-learning is capable of detecting and classifying ships at the instance level, which is hampered by the lack of datasets with multi-class fine-grained annotations and the accompanying ship detection methods. To tackle these issues, we build a new multi-class fine-grained SAR ship dataset ISSC and an efficient multi-scale model for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification. The ISSC dataset has a number of 2764 SAR images and 8466 ship instances, with five categories. With this high-quality dataset that can reflect the diversity of ship scales, we further propose an efficient network for fine-grained SAR ship detection and classification (the ISSC model), which uses overlapping pyramid vision Transformer as the model backbone to extract multi-scale feature maps that prioritize the continuous global information. Subsequently, based on convolution units, two neck structures are designed to refine the directional features representing various multi-class ships and amplify the local semantic information effect of the feature cascading process, respectively. As such, the ISSC model is a novel global-to-local and direction-aware ship detection network for SAR images. Experiments on the ISSC dataset and the single-class ship dataset HRSID and SSDD demonstrate the superior performance of the ISSC model, which has a lower parameter count and achieves substantial improvements, compared to state-of-the-art models. Overall, we have demonstrated the feasibility of combining SAR imaging with the modern deep learning approach for fine-grained ship classification.

item.title: LandSegmenter: Towards a flexible foundation model for Land Use and Land Cover mapping
item.dc:creator: Chenying Liu
Wei Huang
Xiao Xiang Zhu
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Land Use and Land Cover (LULC) mapping is a fundamental task in Earth Observation (EO). However, current LULC models are typically developed for a specific modality and a fixed class taxonomy, limiting their generalizability and broader applicability. Recent advances in foundation models (FMs) offer promising opportunities for building universal models. Yet, task-agnostic FMs often require fine-tuning for downstream applications, whereas task-specific FMs rely on massive amounts of labeled data for training, which is costly and impractical in the remote sensing (RS) domain. To address these challenges, we propose LandSegmenter, an LULC FM framework that resolves three-stage challenges at the input, model, and output levels. From the input side, to alleviate the heavy demand on labeled data for FM training, we introduce LAnd Segment (LAS), a large-scale, multi-modal, multi-source dataset built primarily with globally sampled weak labels from existing LULC products. LAS provides a scalable, cost-effective alternative to manual annotation, enabling large-scale FM training across diverse LULC domains. For model architecture, LandSegmenter integrates an RS-specific adapter for cross-modal feature extraction and a text encoder for semantic awareness enhancement. At the output stage, we introduce a class-wise confidence-guided fusion strategy to mitigate semantic omissions and further improve LandSegmenter’s zero-shot performance. We evaluate LandSegmenter on six precisely annotated LULC datasets spanning diverse modalities and class taxonomies. Extensive transfer learning and zero-shot experiments demonstrate that LandSegmenter achieves competitive or superior performance, particularly in zero-shot settings when transferred to unseen datasets. These results highlight the efficacy of our proposed framework and the utility of weak supervision for building task-specific FMs. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/zhu-xlab/LandSegmenter.git . Published: 2026-05-19T13:39:58+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.818 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chenying Liu; Wei Huang; Xiao Xiang Zhu

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.056

Relevance: 0.818 (must_read)

Land Use and Land Cover (LULC) mapping is a fundamental task in Earth Observation (EO). However, current LULC models are typically developed for a specific modality and a fixed class taxonomy, limiting their generalizability and broader applicability. Recent advances in foundation models (FMs) offer promising opportunities for building universal models. Yet, task-agnostic FMs often require fine-tuning for downstream applications, whereas task-specific FMs rely on massive amounts of labeled data for training, which is costly and impractical in the remote sensing (RS) domain. To address these challenges, we propose LandSegmenter, an LULC FM framework that resolves three-stage challenges at the input, model, and output levels. From the input side, to alleviate the heavy demand on labeled data for FM training, we introduce LAnd Segment (LAS), a large-scale, multi-modal, multi-source dataset built primarily with globally sampled weak labels from existing LULC products. LAS provides a scalable, cost-effective alternative to manual annotation, enabling large-scale FM training across diverse LULC domains. For model architecture, LandSegmenter integrates an RS-specific adapter for cross-modal feature extraction and a text encoder for semantic awareness enhancement. At the output stage, we introduce a class-wise confidence-guided fusion strategy to mitigate semantic omissions and further improve LandSegmenter’s zero-shot performance. We evaluate LandSegmenter on six precisely annotated LULC datasets spanning diverse modalities and class taxonomies. Extensive transfer learning and zero-shot experiments demonstrate that LandSegmenter achieves competitive or superior performance, particularly in zero-shot settings when transferred to unseen datasets. These results highlight the efficacy of our proposed framework and the utility of weak supervision for building task-specific FMs. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/zhu-xlab/LandSegmenter.git .

item.title: MDDS-Net: Dual-Backbone Network With Dynamic Enhancement for SAR Ship Detection in Complex Maritime Environments
item.dc:creator: Weimin Kong
Yun Zhang
Long Li
Niezipeng Kang
Qinglong Hua
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images is a key technology for maritime surveillance.However, practical applications face severe physical challenges, including low signal-to-clutter and signal-to-noise ratios under high sea states, discontinuous scattering of weak targets, and multi-target occlusion in dense ports. Existing methods struggle to suppress these severe interferences while preserving weak target structures. To address these issues, this paper proposes a detection framework based on a dual-backbone complementary coordination mechanism. The method employs a parallel-designed local and global encoder to extract fine-grained spatial details of ships and model long-range scene dependencies, respectively. To explicitly tackle the aforementioned physical degradations, two core modules are introduced. First, a geometric prior-guided multi-scale feature enhancement module (GPMS-FEM) integrates ship shape priors into spatial attention to aggregate discrete scattering centers, addressing weak target discontinuity and occlusion. Second, a bidirectional gated feature fusion module (BG-FFM) enables adaptive cross-backbone feature decoupling to suppress severe sea clutter in low SNR/SCR environments. Additionally, an adaptive tiling strategy guided by land-sea masks is employed to efficiently process large-scale SAR images. Experiments demonstrate that MDDS-Net achieves superior performance on the HRSID and RSDD-SAR datasets. Cross-validation using GF-3 satellite data and Automatic Identification System data further validates the robustness and practical value of our approach under complex maritime conditions. Published: 2026-05-19T19:50:10+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Weimin Kong; Yun Zhang; Long Li; Niezipeng Kang; Qinglong Hua

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3694495

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images is a key technology for maritime surveillance.However, practical applications face severe physical challenges, including low signal-to-clutter and signal-to-noise ratios under high sea states, discontinuous scattering of weak targets, and multi-target occlusion in dense ports. Existing methods struggle to suppress these severe interferences while preserving weak target structures. To address these issues, this paper proposes a detection framework based on a dual-backbone complementary coordination mechanism. The method employs a parallel-designed local and global encoder to extract fine-grained spatial details of ships and model long-range scene dependencies, respectively. To explicitly tackle the aforementioned physical degradations, two core modules are introduced. First, a geometric prior-guided multi-scale feature enhancement module (GPMS-FEM) integrates ship shape priors into spatial attention to aggregate discrete scattering centers, addressing weak target discontinuity and occlusion. Second, a bidirectional gated feature fusion module (BG-FFM) enables adaptive cross-backbone feature decoupling to suppress severe sea clutter in low SNR/SCR environments. Additionally, an adaptive tiling strategy guided by land-sea masks is employed to efficiently process large-scale SAR images. Experiments demonstrate that MDDS-Net achieves superior performance on the HRSID and RSDD-SAR datasets. Cross-validation using GF-3 satellite data and Automatic Identification System data further validates the robustness and practical value of our approach under complex maritime conditions.

item.title: FSGNet: A Frequency-Aware and Semantic Guidance Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Yingmei Zhang
Wangtao Bao
Yong Yang
Weiguo Wan
Qin Xiao
Zhen Zhang
Xueting Zou
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) aims to identify and distinguish small targets from complex backgrounds. Leveraging the powerful multi-scale feature fusion capability of the U-Net architecture, IRSTD has achieved significant progress. However, U-Net suffers from semantic degradation when transferring high-level features from deep to shallow layers, limiting the precise localization of small targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes FSGNet, a lightweight and effective detection framework incorporating frequency-aware and semantic guidance mechanisms. Specifically, a multi-directional interactive attention module is proposed throughout the encoder to capture fine-grained and directional features, enhancing the network’s sensitivity to small, low-contrast targets. To suppress background interference propagated through skip connections, a multi-scale frequency-aware module leverages Fast Fourier transform to filter out target-similar clutter while preserving salient target structures. At the deepest layer, a global pooling module captures high-level semantic information, which is subsequently upsampled and propagated to each decoder stage through the global semantic guidance flows, ensuring semantic consistency and precise localization across scales. Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets demonstrate that FSGNet achieves superior detection performance and maintains high efficiency, highlighting its practical applicability and robustness. The codes will be released on https://github.com/Wangtao-Bao/FSGNet . Published: 2026-05-19T15:18:51+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.812 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yingmei Zhang; Wangtao Bao; Yong Yang; Weiguo Wan; Qin Xiao; Zhen Zhang; Xueting Zou

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132925

Relevance: 0.812 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) aims to identify and distinguish small targets from complex backgrounds. Leveraging the powerful multi-scale feature fusion capability of the U-Net architecture, IRSTD has achieved significant progress. However, U-Net suffers from semantic degradation when transferring high-level features from deep to shallow layers, limiting the precise localization of small targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes FSGNet, a lightweight and effective detection framework incorporating frequency-aware and semantic guidance mechanisms. Specifically, a multi-directional interactive attention module is proposed throughout the encoder to capture fine-grained and directional features, enhancing the network’s sensitivity to small, low-contrast targets. To suppress background interference propagated through skip connections, a multi-scale frequency-aware module leverages Fast Fourier transform to filter out target-similar clutter while preserving salient target structures. At the deepest layer, a global pooling module captures high-level semantic information, which is subsequently upsampled and propagated to each decoder stage through the global semantic guidance flows, ensuring semantic consistency and precise localization across scales. Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets demonstrate that FSGNet achieves superior detection performance and maintains high efficiency, highlighting its practical applicability and robustness. The codes will be released on https://github.com/Wangtao-Bao/FSGNet .

item.title: Deep Unfolding Residual Decomposition for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Fan Hao
Feng Wei
Feng Zhou
Zhipeng Wang
Shichao Yao
Xueyan Yang
Zongfang Ma
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Detecting infrared dim small targets remains a challenging task due to strong background interference and low signal-to-clutter ratio (SCR), which frequently results in target features being submerged by clutter. Furthermore, existing methods often struggle to strike a balance between physical interpretability and advanced feature representation. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Deep Unfolding Residual Decomposition (DURD) method based on a novel dual-branch framework. First, an adaptive structured decomposition (ASD) module is proposed, which unfolds iterative optimization into a deep neural network. The ASD module leverages an implicit low-rank subspace projection and an adaptive structured sparse perception mechanism to separate high-quality physical sparse priors from low-rank background components. Second, to further exploit deep semantic representations and suppress high-frequency clutter, a residual-driven multi-scale Encoder–Decoder (RMED) is designed. Using physically constrained residual features as input, RMED emphasizes target regions while suppressing background interference. Through full-scale feature aggregation and deep supervision mechanisms, it hierarchically extracts robust multi-scale semantic representations. Finally, a dual-branch cooperative fusion (DCF) module is developed to integrate the outputs of both branches. The DCF employs a learnable gating factor to adaptively balance the contributions of physical sparse priors and deep semantic features, thereby achieving a complementary and discriminative enhancement for infrared small target detection. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed DURD significantly enhances the detection accuracy in complex scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/haofan72/DURD. Published: 2026-05-19T19:49:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fan Hao; Feng Wei; Feng Zhou; Zhipeng Wang; Shichao Yao; Xueyan Yang; Zongfang Ma

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3694155

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Detecting infrared dim small targets remains a challenging task due to strong background interference and low signal-to-clutter ratio (SCR), which frequently results in target features being submerged by clutter. Furthermore, existing methods often struggle to strike a balance between physical interpretability and advanced feature representation. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Deep Unfolding Residual Decomposition (DURD) method based on a novel dual-branch framework. First, an adaptive structured decomposition (ASD) module is proposed, which unfolds iterative optimization into a deep neural network. The ASD module leverages an implicit low-rank subspace projection and an adaptive structured sparse perception mechanism to separate high-quality physical sparse priors from low-rank background components. Second, to further exploit deep semantic representations and suppress high-frequency clutter, a residual-driven multi-scale Encoder–Decoder (RMED) is designed. Using physically constrained residual features as input, RMED emphasizes target regions while suppressing background interference. Through full-scale feature aggregation and deep supervision mechanisms, it hierarchically extracts robust multi-scale semantic representations. Finally, a dual-branch cooperative fusion (DCF) module is developed to integrate the outputs of both branches. The DCF employs a learnable gating factor to adaptively balance the contributions of physical sparse priors and deep semantic features, thereby achieving a complementary and discriminative enhancement for infrared small target detection. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed DURD significantly enhances the detection accuracy in complex scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/haofan72/DURD.

item.title: Pose Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Human Body Part Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Arindam Dutta
Rohit Lal
Yash Garg
Calvin-Khang Ta
Dripta S. Raychaudhuri
Amit K. Roy-Chowdhury
item.description: Existing algorithms for human body part segmentation have shown promising results on challenging datasets, primarily relying on end-to-end supervision. However, these algorithms exhibit severe performance drops in the face of domain shifts, leading to inaccurate segmentation masks. To tackle this issue, we introduce POSTURE: Pose Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Human Body Part Segmentation - an innovative pseudo-labelling approach designed to improve segmentation performance on the unlabeled target data. Distinct from conventional domain adaptive methods for general semantic segmentation, POSTURE stands out by considering the underlying structure of the human body and uses anatomical guidance from pose keypoints to drive the adaptation process. This strong inductive prior translates to impressive performance improvements, averaging 8% over existing state-of-the-art domain adaptive semantic segmentation methods across three benchmark datasets. Furthermore, the inherent flexibility of our proposed approach facilitates seamless extension to source-free settings (SF-POSTURE), effectively mitigating potential privacy and computational concerns, with negligible drop in performance. Published: 2026-05-20T19:54:32+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Arindam Dutta; Rohit Lal; Yash Garg; Calvin-Khang Ta; Dripta S. Raychaudhuri; Amit K. Roy-Chowdhury

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3689414

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Existing algorithms for human body part segmentation have shown promising results on challenging datasets, primarily relying on end-to-end supervision. However, these algorithms exhibit severe performance drops in the face of domain shifts, leading to inaccurate segmentation masks. To tackle this issue, we introduce POSTURE: Pose Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Human Body Part Segmentation - an innovative pseudo-labelling approach designed to improve segmentation performance on the unlabeled target data. Distinct from conventional domain adaptive methods for general semantic segmentation, POSTURE stands out by considering the underlying structure of the human body and uses anatomical guidance from pose keypoints to drive the adaptation process. This strong inductive prior translates to impressive performance improvements, averaging 8% over existing state-of-the-art domain adaptive semantic segmentation methods across three benchmark datasets. Furthermore, the inherent flexibility of our proposed approach facilitates seamless extension to source-free settings (SF-POSTURE), effectively mitigating potential privacy and computational concerns, with negligible drop in performance.

item.title: Tiny object detection via implicit feature fusion and hybrid metric adaptive label assignment
item.dc:creator: Qiaochuan Chen
Tianyang Xu
Yan Sun
Bing Wang
Yuexing Han
item.description: Tiny object detection (TOD) has broad applications across various fields, including remote sensing image analysis, autonomous driving, and UAV surveillance. Due to the extremely limited pixels representing tiny objects, existing methods often fail to effectively utilize multi-scale features in detecting tiny objects. Additionally, current label assignment strategies are inadequate to assign a sufficient number of high-quality positive samples for tiny objects, which hinders the model’s ability to accurately identify them. This paper proposes a TOD network based on Implicit Feature Fusion (IFF) and Hybrid Adaptive Label Assignment (HALA), named IHANet, which aims to achieve high-precision detection of tiny objects. IFF mitigates feature misalignment in multi-scale fusion by leveraging implicit neural representations, mapping feature maps from different pyramid levels to a uniform size before fusion. By modeling feature maps as continuous representations, IFF enables effective fusion at any resolution, preserving tiny object details and reducing information loss. HALA improves label assignment by combining IoU with the Receptive Field Distance (RFD), which performs better for tiny objects. HALA then utilizes an adaptive selection strategy to identify high-quality training samples, enhancing model training and detection performance. Extensive experiments on the AI-TOD, SODA-D, VisDrone, and AgriPest datasets demonstrate that IHANet achieves state-of-the-art performance on various TOD scenarios, such as remote sensing, autonomous driving, low-altitude drone imaging, and agricultural imaging, achieving an AP score of 29.1 on the AI-TOD dataset. Published: 2026-05-19T06:54:05+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.805 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qiaochuan Chen; Tianyang Xu; Yan Sun; Bing Wang; Yuexing Han

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116218

Relevance: 0.805 (must_read)

Tiny object detection (TOD) has broad applications across various fields, including remote sensing image analysis, autonomous driving, and UAV surveillance. Due to the extremely limited pixels representing tiny objects, existing methods often fail to effectively utilize multi-scale features in detecting tiny objects. Additionally, current label assignment strategies are inadequate to assign a sufficient number of high-quality positive samples for tiny objects, which hinders the model’s ability to accurately identify them. This paper proposes a TOD network based on Implicit Feature Fusion (IFF) and Hybrid Adaptive Label Assignment (HALA), named IHANet, which aims to achieve high-precision detection of tiny objects. IFF mitigates feature misalignment in multi-scale fusion by leveraging implicit neural representations, mapping feature maps from different pyramid levels to a uniform size before fusion. By modeling feature maps as continuous representations, IFF enables effective fusion at any resolution, preserving tiny object details and reducing information loss. HALA improves label assignment by combining IoU with the Receptive Field Distance (RFD), which performs better for tiny objects. HALA then utilizes an adaptive selection strategy to identify high-quality training samples, enhancing model training and detection performance. Extensive experiments on the AI-TOD, SODA-D, VisDrone, and AgriPest datasets demonstrate that IHANet achieves state-of-the-art performance on various TOD scenarios, such as remote sensing, autonomous driving, low-altitude drone imaging, and agricultural imaging, achieving an AP score of 29.1 on the AI-TOD dataset.

item.title: Semantics Disentanglement and Composition for Universal Image Coding with Efficiently LLM Reasoning and Generative Diffusion
item.dc:creator: Jinming Liu
Yuntao Wei
Junyan Lin
Heming Sun
Shengyang Zhao
Zhibo Chen
Wenjun Zeng
Xin Jin
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Learned image compression methods have shown impressive performance but are often highly specialized for either human perception or specific machine vision tasks. This specialization limits their versatility and requires costly retraining for new applications. To address this, we introduce UniCodec, a universal codec built on a novel paradigm of semantic disentanglement at the encoder and compositional generation at the decoder. This framework is designed to simultaneously serve both human and machine needs, eliminating the need for task-specific retraining. At the encoder, UniCodec leverages pre-generated, task-specific label codebooks created by a Large Language Model (LLM). For any given task, a grounding model uses the corresponding codebook to perform task-aware disentanglement, compressing only the most relevant image regions. This mechanism not only saves significant bits but is also the key to our system’s rapid, zero-retraining adaptation: switching to a new task is as simple as selecting a new codebook. The decoder then performs compositional generation: it combines the compact, disentangled components with powerful priors from a generative diffusion model. This process reconstructs a high-quality, complete image optimized with rich detail for human perception and precise features for machine vision tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniCodec consistently outperforms existing methods, effectively bridging the gap between human-centric and machine-centric compression. Published: 2026-05-20T19:54:18+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinming Liu; Yuntao Wei; Junyan Lin; Heming Sun; Shengyang Zhao; Zhibo Chen; Wenjun Zeng; Xin Jin

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3695062

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

Learned image compression methods have shown impressive performance but are often highly specialized for either human perception or specific machine vision tasks. This specialization limits their versatility and requires costly retraining for new applications. To address this, we introduce UniCodec, a universal codec built on a novel paradigm of semantic disentanglement at the encoder and compositional generation at the decoder. This framework is designed to simultaneously serve both human and machine needs, eliminating the need for task-specific retraining. At the encoder, UniCodec leverages pre-generated, task-specific label codebooks created by a Large Language Model (LLM). For any given task, a grounding model uses the corresponding codebook to perform task-aware disentanglement, compressing only the most relevant image regions. This mechanism not only saves significant bits but is also the key to our system’s rapid, zero-retraining adaptation: switching to a new task is as simple as selecting a new codebook. The decoder then performs compositional generation: it combines the compact, disentangled components with powerful priors from a generative diffusion model. This process reconstructs a high-quality, complete image optimized with rich detail for human perception and precise features for machine vision tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniCodec consistently outperforms existing methods, effectively bridging the gap between human-centric and machine-centric compression.

item.title: RERF-net: Rotation-equivariant enhancement and refined fusion network for multi-modal 3D detection
item.dc:creator: Di Tian
Jiahang Shi
Jiabo Li
Jiawei Wang
item.description: Multi-modal 3D object detection enables high-precision detection via fusing LiDAR geometric and camera semantic information. However, this study finds that factors such as camera viewpoint variations easily leading to feature representation distortion and the difficulty in balancing modality-specific and shared information significantly impact detection performance. To resolve the issue of feature distortion caused by camera viewpoint changes, this paper proposes a Rotation-Equivariant Convolution Module (RECM). It ensures that rotation equivariant enhancement does not disrupt the original feature flow through channel and scale adaptation, thereby improving the rotation equivariance of image features and mitigating feature distortion in multi-view scenarios. Secondly, to tackle the challenge of balancing modality-specific and shared information, which often leads to feature redundancy and insufficient exploitation of complementarity, this paper introduces a Dual-Feature Dynamic Refined Fusion Module (DFDRFM). For the specific features of images and point clouds, this module captures global and local correlations through multi-scale attention and subsequently filters redundant information via a gating mechanism, outputting enhanced shared features to strengthen modality complementarity. Building on these, this paper combines the RECM and the DFDRFM to propose a multi-sensor fusion network integrating LiDAR and cameras, termed RERF-Net. Compared to baseline methods, RERF-Net achieves a 1.6% improvement in the NDS metric and a 2.1% improvement in the mAP metric on the nuScenes dataset. These experimental results demonstrate the validity of the proposed algorithms, which are capable of improving both the accuracy and robustness. The code is set on https://github.com/sjh1213984445-commits/RERF-NET.git . Published: 2026-05-20T19:36:43+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.800 (must_read)

Authors: Di Tian; Jiahang Shi; Jiabo Li; Jiawei Wang

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116255

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Multi-modal 3D object detection enables high-precision detection via fusing LiDAR geometric and camera semantic information. However, this study finds that factors such as camera viewpoint variations easily leading to feature representation distortion and the difficulty in balancing modality-specific and shared information significantly impact detection performance. To resolve the issue of feature distortion caused by camera viewpoint changes, this paper proposes a Rotation-Equivariant Convolution Module (RECM). It ensures that rotation equivariant enhancement does not disrupt the original feature flow through channel and scale adaptation, thereby improving the rotation equivariance of image features and mitigating feature distortion in multi-view scenarios. Secondly, to tackle the challenge of balancing modality-specific and shared information, which often leads to feature redundancy and insufficient exploitation of complementarity, this paper introduces a Dual-Feature Dynamic Refined Fusion Module (DFDRFM). For the specific features of images and point clouds, this module captures global and local correlations through multi-scale attention and subsequently filters redundant information via a gating mechanism, outputting enhanced shared features to strengthen modality complementarity. Building on these, this paper combines the RECM and the DFDRFM to propose a multi-sensor fusion network integrating LiDAR and cameras, termed RERF-Net. Compared to baseline methods, RERF-Net achieves a 1.6% improvement in the NDS metric and a 2.1% improvement in the mAP metric on the nuScenes dataset. These experimental results demonstrate the validity of the proposed algorithms, which are capable of improving both the accuracy and robustness. The code is set on https://github.com/sjh1213984445-commits/RERF-NET.git .

item.title: DCH-Net: A hyperspectral object detection network with differential convolution and spectral gradient fusion
item.dc:creator: Ailin Niu
Xinyu Yan
Qiang Wang
Jiuchen Chen
Xiaolin Han
Qizhi Xu
item.description: Hyperspectral remote sensing images provide rich spectral information, making them widely used in downstream tasks such as object detection. However, existing detection methods often struggle to capture local variations along the spectral dimension, limiting the full exploitation of spectral information. To address this issue, we propose D ifferential C onvolution-based H yperspectral detection Net work (DCH-Net) to effectively exploit the differential information in hyperspectral images. First, we design a Dual Path Diverse Mixture of Experts fusion module to dynamically integrate the original channels with differential information. Second, we develop an Adaptive Deformable Attention mechanism that incorporates the image content of reference points into sampling position prediction, enabling effective use of local contextual information. In addition, considering the limited quantity and scene coverage of existing hyperspectral object detection datasets, we construct a hyperspectral ship detection dataset named SHD-25. A spectral-correlation-based target enhancement strategy is also introduced to further improve small target detectability. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches across multiple benchmarks, indicating its effectiveness for hyperspectral object detection. The source code and dataset will be publicly available at https://github.com/nal793/DCH-Net . Published: 2026-05-20T15:53:21+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ailin Niu; Xinyu Yan; Qiang Wang; Jiuchen Chen; Xiaolin Han; Qizhi Xu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113956

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Hyperspectral remote sensing images provide rich spectral information, making them widely used in downstream tasks such as object detection. However, existing detection methods often struggle to capture local variations along the spectral dimension, limiting the full exploitation of spectral information. To address this issue, we propose D ifferential C onvolution-based H yperspectral detection Net work (DCH-Net) to effectively exploit the differential information in hyperspectral images. First, we design a Dual Path Diverse Mixture of Experts fusion module to dynamically integrate the original channels with differential information. Second, we develop an Adaptive Deformable Attention mechanism that incorporates the image content of reference points into sampling position prediction, enabling effective use of local contextual information. In addition, considering the limited quantity and scene coverage of existing hyperspectral object detection datasets, we construct a hyperspectral ship detection dataset named SHD-25. A spectral-correlation-based target enhancement strategy is also introduced to further improve small target detectability. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches across multiple benchmarks, indicating its effectiveness for hyperspectral object detection. The source code and dataset will be publicly available at https://github.com/nal793/DCH-Net .

item.title: BrushEdit: All-In-One Image Inpainting and Editing
item.dc:creator: Yaowei Li
Yuxuan Bian
Xuan Ju
Zhaoyang Zhang
Junhao Zhuang
Ying Shan
Yuexian Zou
Qiang Xu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Image editing has advanced significantly with the development of diffusion models using both inversion-based and instruction-based methods. However, current inversion-based approaches struggle with big modifications (e.g., adding or removing objects) due to the structured nature of inversion noise, which hinders substantial changes. Meanwhile, instruction-based methods often constrain users to black-box operations, limiting direct interaction for specifying editing regions and intensity. To address these limitations, we propose BrushEdit, a novel inpainting-based instruction-guided image editing paradigm, which leverages multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and image inpainting models to enable autonomous, user-friendly, and interactive free-form instruction editing. Specifically, we devise a system enabling free-form instruction editing by integrating MLLMs and a dual-branch image inpainting model in an agentcooperative framework to perform editing category classification, main object identification, mask acquisition, and editing area inpainting. Extensive experiments show that our framework effectively combines MLLMs and inpainting models, achieving superior performance across seven metrics including mask region preservation and editing effect coherence. Published: 2026-05-19T19:49:41+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.799 (must_read)

Authors: Yaowei Li; Yuxuan Bian; Xuan Ju; Zhaoyang Zhang; Junhao Zhuang; Ying Shan; Yuexian Zou; Qiang Xu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3695011

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Image editing has advanced significantly with the development of diffusion models using both inversion-based and instruction-based methods. However, current inversion-based approaches struggle with big modifications (e.g., adding or removing objects) due to the structured nature of inversion noise, which hinders substantial changes. Meanwhile, instruction-based methods often constrain users to black-box operations, limiting direct interaction for specifying editing regions and intensity. To address these limitations, we propose BrushEdit, a novel inpainting-based instruction-guided image editing paradigm, which leverages multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and image inpainting models to enable autonomous, user-friendly, and interactive free-form instruction editing. Specifically, we devise a system enabling free-form instruction editing by integrating MLLMs and a dual-branch image inpainting model in an agentcooperative framework to perform editing category classification, main object identification, mask acquisition, and editing area inpainting. Extensive experiments show that our framework effectively combines MLLMs and inpainting models, achieving superior performance across seven metrics including mask region preservation and editing effect coherence.

item.title: ASD-YOLO: An Adaptive-Downsampling and Spatial Pyramid Path–Enhanced Network for Multi-Scale Aircraft Detection in SAR Images
item.dc:creator: Jiabin Hu
Zejun Zhang
Chunqiang Min
Peiqi Zhang
Jianxiao Xie
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Aircraft detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery remains challenging due to scale variation, speckle noise, background clutter, and the loss of fine structural cues during downsampling. To address these issues, we propose ASD-YOLO, an enhanced YOLO-based detector that strengthens detail preservation, multiscale feature consistency, and high-IoU localization. The framework incorporates three lightweight modules: (1) an Adaptive Downsampling (ADown) block that mitigates aliasing and retains small-target edges; (2) a Spatial Pyramid Path-Enhanced Lightweight Aggregation Network (SP-PELAN) that improves cross-scale semantic alignment; and (3) a Downsampled Adaptive-Kernel Convolution (DS AKConv) head that couples adaptive kernels with complementary pooling to enhance localization robustness under clutter. Experiments on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 and SADD show that ASD-YOLO consistently surpasses YOLO-based baselines and other advanced detectors, achieving clear gains in mAP@0.5, mAP@0.5:0.95, and F1-score while keeping a compact complexity (9.9M params, 28.5 GFLOPs) and fast inference (168.9 FPS). These results indicate that ASD-YOLO is an effective and concise solution for multi-scale aircraft detection in SAR imagery. Published: 2026-05-19T19:52:25+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiabin Hu; Zejun Zhang; Chunqiang Min; Peiqi Zhang; Jianxiao Xie

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3694932

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Aircraft detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery remains challenging due to scale variation, speckle noise, background clutter, and the loss of fine structural cues during downsampling. To address these issues, we propose ASD-YOLO, an enhanced YOLO-based detector that strengthens detail preservation, multiscale feature consistency, and high-IoU localization. The framework incorporates three lightweight modules: (1) an Adaptive Downsampling (ADown) block that mitigates aliasing and retains small-target edges; (2) a Spatial Pyramid Path-Enhanced Lightweight Aggregation Network (SP-PELAN) that improves cross-scale semantic alignment; and (3) a Downsampled Adaptive-Kernel Convolution (DS AKConv) head that couples adaptive kernels with complementary pooling to enhance localization robustness under clutter. Experiments on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 and SADD show that ASD-YOLO consistently surpasses YOLO-based baselines and other advanced detectors, achieving clear gains in mAP@0.5, mAP@0.5:0.95, and F1-score while keeping a compact complexity (9.9M params, 28.5 GFLOPs) and fast inference (168.9 FPS). These results indicate that ASD-YOLO is an effective and concise solution for multi-scale aircraft detection in SAR imagery.

item.title: RIVDCB-UNet: a robust vessel detection in cluttered backgrounds via UNet for SDGSAT-1 thermal infrared data
item.dc:creator: Kun Li
Tao Wang
Hongzhao Tang
Zhongchang Sun
Dailiang Peng
Yonggang Qian
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Aiming at complex and variable scenarios with cloud and fog obscuration and diverse interference sources, a robust infrared vessel detection in cluttered backgrounds via the UNet (RIVDCB-UNet) is designed. This model effectively overcomes the limitations of traditional UNet in small target detection and significantly improves its detection performance. The RIVDCB-UNet model takes a single thermal infrared image as input and incorporates the BCE-Dice loss function to ensure stability and accuracy during the training process. The model integrates the EfficientNet backbone network for efficient feature extraction, followed by feature upsampling and fusion through a U-shaped decoder. Finally, an eight-connected neighborhood clustering module is introduced to further refine pixel-level classification results and achieve precise localization. The training of the proposed model leverages the publicly available NUDT-SIRST-Sea Dataset and our self-constructed SDGSAT-1 thermal infrared vessel detection dataset. Data augmentation techniques, including rotation and cropping, are employed to expand the dataset, thereby addressing the issue of target-background imbalance in the original datasets. This imbalance can cause the model to overly focus on information-sparse background regions. The proposed model achieves detection rates, recall rates, and F1 scores of 95.39%, 97.89%, 96.62% on the NUDT-SIRST-Sea Dataset, and 97.81%, 96.78%, 97.29% on the SDGSAT-1 dataset, respectively. These results are significantly higher than those of other models such as ACM (84.81%, 73.17%, 78.56% on NUDT-SIRST-Sea and 90.17%, 72.87%, 80.60% on SDGSAT-1) and DNANet (89.98%, 83.10%, 86.40% on NUDT-SIRST-Sea and 95.64%, 88.21%, 91.77% on SDGSAT-1). Published: 2026-05-19T19:49:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kun Li; Tao Wang; Hongzhao Tang; Zhongchang Sun; Dailiang Peng; Yonggang Qian

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3694901

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Aiming at complex and variable scenarios with cloud and fog obscuration and diverse interference sources, a robust infrared vessel detection in cluttered backgrounds via the UNet (RIVDCB-UNet) is designed. This model effectively overcomes the limitations of traditional UNet in small target detection and significantly improves its detection performance. The RIVDCB-UNet model takes a single thermal infrared image as input and incorporates the BCE-Dice loss function to ensure stability and accuracy during the training process. The model integrates the EfficientNet backbone network for efficient feature extraction, followed by feature upsampling and fusion through a U-shaped decoder. Finally, an eight-connected neighborhood clustering module is introduced to further refine pixel-level classification results and achieve precise localization. The training of the proposed model leverages the publicly available NUDT-SIRST-Sea Dataset and our self-constructed SDGSAT-1 thermal infrared vessel detection dataset. Data augmentation techniques, including rotation and cropping, are employed to expand the dataset, thereby addressing the issue of target-background imbalance in the original datasets. This imbalance can cause the model to overly focus on information-sparse background regions. The proposed model achieves detection rates, recall rates, and F1 scores of 95.39%, 97.89%, 96.62% on the NUDT-SIRST-Sea Dataset, and 97.81%, 96.78%, 97.29% on the SDGSAT-1 dataset, respectively. These results are significantly higher than those of other models such as ACM (84.81%, 73.17%, 78.56% on NUDT-SIRST-Sea and 90.17%, 72.87%, 80.60% on SDGSAT-1) and DNANet (89.98%, 83.10%, 86.40% on NUDT-SIRST-Sea and 95.64%, 88.21%, 91.77% on SDGSAT-1).

item.title: GeoMamba: A Geometry-driven MambaVision Framework and Dataset for Fine-grained Optical-SAR Object Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Tiantong Fang
Xiuwei Wang
Jing Xiao
Wujie Zhou
Liang Liao
Mi Wang
item.description: Multi-source remote sensing enables complementary observation of ground objects, while cross-modal fine-grained object retrieval remains challenging, especially under unaligned optical and SAR conditions. Unlike conventional retrieval settings that rely on paired or spatially aligned samples, practical optical-SAR retrieval is affected by substantial modality discrepancy, speckle noise, and structural inconsistency, which limit robust cross-modal representation learning. To address this problem, we propose GeoMamba, a geometry-driven framework tailored for optical-SAR fine-grained retrieval. Specifically, GeoMamba introduces a Geometric Feature Injection (GFI) module that enhances cross-modal feature interaction and incorporates structural priors, thereby improving the robustness of SAR representations and promoting geometry-consistent feature learning. In addition, a Geometric Consistency Constraint (GCC) module, together with a Deep Supervision (DS) strategy, imposes hierarchical geometric constraints using classical operators, which helps preserve informative object structures during representation learning. We further construct a new dataset, FGOS-as, containing 11 aerospace and maritime categories for evaluating unaligned cross-modal fine-grained object retrieval in realistic remote sensing scenarios. Extensive experiments on FGOS-as demonstrate that GeoMamba outperforms existing methods, achieving 63.3% mAP and 77.0% Rank-1 accuracy in all-to-all retrieval setting. Published: 2026-05-19T12:08:09+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tiantong Fang; Xiuwei Wang; Jing Xiao; Wujie Zhou; Liang Liao; Mi Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Multi-source remote sensing enables complementary observation of ground objects, while cross-modal fine-grained object retrieval remains challenging, especially under unaligned optical and SAR conditions. Unlike conventional retrieval settings that rely on paired or spatially aligned samples, practical optical-SAR retrieval is affected by substantial modality discrepancy, speckle noise, and structural inconsistency, which limit robust cross-modal representation learning. To address this problem, we propose GeoMamba, a geometry-driven framework tailored for optical-SAR fine-grained retrieval. Specifically, GeoMamba introduces a Geometric Feature Injection (GFI) module that enhances cross-modal feature interaction and incorporates structural priors, thereby improving the robustness of SAR representations and promoting geometry-consistent feature learning. In addition, a Geometric Consistency Constraint (GCC) module, together with a Deep Supervision (DS) strategy, imposes hierarchical geometric constraints using classical operators, which helps preserve informative object structures during representation learning. We further construct a new dataset, FGOS-as, containing 11 aerospace and maritime categories for evaluating unaligned cross-modal fine-grained object retrieval in realistic remote sensing scenarios. Extensive experiments on FGOS-as demonstrate that GeoMamba outperforms existing methods, achieving 63.3% mAP and 77.0% Rank-1 accuracy in all-to-all retrieval setting.

item.title: Open-set Anomaly Segmentation in Complex Scenarios
item.dc:creator: Song Xia
Yi Yu
Henghui Ding
Wenhan Yang
Shifei Liu
Alex C. Kot
Xudong Jiang
item.description: Precise segmentation of out-of-distribution (OoD) objects, herein referred to as anomalies, is crucial for the reliable deployment of semantic segmentation models in open-set, safety-critical applications, such as autonomous driving. Current anomalous segmentation benchmarks predominantly focus on favorable weather conditions, resulting in untrustworthy evaluations that overlook the risks posed by diverse meteorological conditions in open-set environments, such as low illumination, dense fog, and heavy rain. To bridge this gap, this paper introduces the ComSAmy, a Complex Scenarios Anomaly segmentation benchmark. ComSAmy encompasses a wide spectrum of adverse weather conditions, dynamic driving environments, and diverse anomaly types to comprehensively evaluate the model performance in realistic open-world scenarios. Our extensive evaluation of several state-of-the-art anomalous segmentation models reveals that existing methods demonstrate significant deficiencies in such challenging scenarios, highlighting their serious safety risks for real-world deployment. To solve that, we propose a novel energy-entropy learning (EEL) strategy that integrates the complementary information from energy and entropy to bolster the robustness of anomaly segmentation under complex open-world environments. Additionally, a diffusion-based anomalous training data synthesizer is proposed to generate diverse and high-quality anomalous images to enhance the existing copy-paste training data synthesizer. Extensive experimental results on both public and ComSAmy benchmarks demonstrate that our proposed diffusion-based synthesizer with energy and entropy learning (DiffEEL) framework serves as an effective and generalizable plug-and-play method to enhance existing models, yielding an average improvement of around 4.96% in AUPRC and 9.87% in FPR95. Published: 2026-05-19T19:51:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Song Xia; Yi Yu; Henghui Ding; Wenhan Yang; Shifei Liu; Alex C. Kot; Xudong Jiang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3693134

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Precise segmentation of out-of-distribution (OoD) objects, herein referred to as anomalies, is crucial for the reliable deployment of semantic segmentation models in open-set, safety-critical applications, such as autonomous driving. Current anomalous segmentation benchmarks predominantly focus on favorable weather conditions, resulting in untrustworthy evaluations that overlook the risks posed by diverse meteorological conditions in open-set environments, such as low illumination, dense fog, and heavy rain. To bridge this gap, this paper introduces the ComSAmy, a Complex Scenarios Anomaly segmentation benchmark. ComSAmy encompasses a wide spectrum of adverse weather conditions, dynamic driving environments, and diverse anomaly types to comprehensively evaluate the model performance in realistic open-world scenarios. Our extensive evaluation of several state-of-the-art anomalous segmentation models reveals that existing methods demonstrate significant deficiencies in such challenging scenarios, highlighting their serious safety risks for real-world deployment. To solve that, we propose a novel energy-entropy learning (EEL) strategy that integrates the complementary information from energy and entropy to bolster the robustness of anomaly segmentation under complex open-world environments. Additionally, a diffusion-based anomalous training data synthesizer is proposed to generate diverse and high-quality anomalous images to enhance the existing copy-paste training data synthesizer. Extensive experimental results on both public and ComSAmy benchmarks demonstrate that our proposed diffusion-based synthesizer with energy and entropy learning (DiffEEL) framework serves as an effective and generalizable plug-and-play method to enhance existing models, yielding an average improvement of around 4.96% in AUPRC and 9.87% in FPR95.

item.title: SkyNative: A Native Multimodal Framework for Remote Sensing Visual Evidence Reasoning
item.dc:creator: Xiao Yang
Ronghao Fu
Zhiwen Lin
Zhuoran Duan
Jiashun Zhu
Jiasen Hu
Lang Sun
Weipeng Zhang
Jiaqi Liu
Xu Na
Haoran Liu
Weijie Zhang
Bo Yang
item.description: Remote sensing vision-language models commonly rely on pretrained visual encoders to convert images into semantic features before language-model reasoning. While effective for scene-level understanding, this pipeline may prematurely compress local visual evidence, making fine-grained spatial reasoning vulnerable to language priors, especially in ultra-high-resolution remote sensing imagery. We present SkyNative, a native multimodal framework for remote sensing that adopts an encoder-free architecture, removing the pretrained visual backbone to directly represent images as raw patch tokens in the language-model token space. To reconcile low-level visual patches with textual tokens, SkyNative introduces a modality-aware decoupling mechanism that uses modality-specific parameters within a unified autoregressive backbone. We further introduce a visual reliance benchmark that diagnoses whether models ground their answers in image evidence through progressive visual degradation and misleading textual prompts. Across standard remote sensing understanding tasks and large-format spatial reasoning evaluations, SkyNative shows stronger image-grounded perception and improved robustness against prompt-induced language priors. These results suggest that native patch-level multimodal modeling is a promising direction for reliable remote sensing vision-language reasoning. Published: 2026-05-18T07:06:23+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiao Yang; Ronghao Fu; Zhiwen Lin; Zhuoran Duan; Jiashun Zhu; Jiasen Hu; Lang Sun; Weipeng Zhang; Jiaqi Liu; Xu Na; Haoran Liu; Weijie Zhang; Bo Yang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Remote sensing vision-language models commonly rely on pretrained visual encoders to convert images into semantic features before language-model reasoning. While effective for scene-level understanding, this pipeline may prematurely compress local visual evidence, making fine-grained spatial reasoning vulnerable to language priors, especially in ultra-high-resolution remote sensing imagery. We present SkyNative, a native multimodal framework for remote sensing that adopts an encoder-free architecture, removing the pretrained visual backbone to directly represent images as raw patch tokens in the language-model token space. To reconcile low-level visual patches with textual tokens, SkyNative introduces a modality-aware decoupling mechanism that uses modality-specific parameters within a unified autoregressive backbone. We further introduce a visual reliance benchmark that diagnoses whether models ground their answers in image evidence through progressive visual degradation and misleading textual prompts. Across standard remote sensing understanding tasks and large-format spatial reasoning evaluations, SkyNative shows stronger image-grounded perception and improved robustness against prompt-induced language priors. These results suggest that native patch-level multimodal modeling is a promising direction for reliable remote sensing vision-language reasoning.

item.title: Bridging 3D Geometry and 1D Temporal Signals: A Symmetric Masked Pre-training Framework for Heterogeneous Multimodal Fusion
item.dc:creator: Hui Zhang
Zhongyang Zhang
Jian Li
Ting Ren
Lanfeng Huang
Zhen Li
Yiyan Guan
Zhipeng Li
Minxue Liang
Peng Zhang
item.description: Effective fusion of heterogeneous modalities—specifically static 3D geometric structures and dynamic 1D temporal signals—is fundamental to modeling physical and biological systems where ”structure constrains function.” However, bridging the semantic gap between spatial topology and temporal dynamics remains a formidable challenge, further exacerbated by the scarcity of labeled data in specialized domains. To address these limitations, we propose GTCF-Net, a novel geometric-temporal fusion framework empowered by a symmetric masked pre-training paradigm. First, to overcome the data scarcity bottleneck, we introduce a dual-stream self-supervised learning strategy: a Masked Point Modeling (MPM) task learns robust topological priors from large-scale shape repositories, while a Masked Signal Modeling (MSM) task captures intrinsic temporal dependencies from massive time-series corpora. This symmetric mechanism effectively transfers generalized priors to downstream tasks. Second, to align these distinct modalities, we design a Geometry-Guided Dynamics Attention (GGDA) module that explicitly leverages fine-grained geometric tokens to query relevant temporal states, achieving deep bidirectional alignment. We instantiate and validate this framework on the challenging task of Epilepsy Cognitive Assessment, where hippocampal atrophy (geometry) is fundamentally associated with brain network disruptions (dynamics). Extensive experiments demonstrate that GTCF-Net achieves highly competitive performance against advanced baselines, confirming the framework’s ability to identify meaningful structural-functional couplings, establishing it as a promising framework for multimodal representation learning. Published: 2026-05-19T06:57:52+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hui Zhang; Zhongyang Zhang; Jian Li; Ting Ren; Lanfeng Huang; Zhen Li; Yiyan Guan; Zhipeng Li; Minxue Liang; Peng Zhang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104475

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Effective fusion of heterogeneous modalities—specifically static 3D geometric structures and dynamic 1D temporal signals—is fundamental to modeling physical and biological systems where ”structure constrains function.” However, bridging the semantic gap between spatial topology and temporal dynamics remains a formidable challenge, further exacerbated by the scarcity of labeled data in specialized domains. To address these limitations, we propose GTCF-Net, a novel geometric-temporal fusion framework empowered by a symmetric masked pre-training paradigm. First, to overcome the data scarcity bottleneck, we introduce a dual-stream self-supervised learning strategy: a Masked Point Modeling (MPM) task learns robust topological priors from large-scale shape repositories, while a Masked Signal Modeling (MSM) task captures intrinsic temporal dependencies from massive time-series corpora. This symmetric mechanism effectively transfers generalized priors to downstream tasks. Second, to align these distinct modalities, we design a Geometry-Guided Dynamics Attention (GGDA) module that explicitly leverages fine-grained geometric tokens to query relevant temporal states, achieving deep bidirectional alignment. We instantiate and validate this framework on the challenging task of Epilepsy Cognitive Assessment, where hippocampal atrophy (geometry) is fundamentally associated with brain network disruptions (dynamics). Extensive experiments demonstrate that GTCF-Net achieves highly competitive performance against advanced baselines, confirming the framework’s ability to identify meaningful structural-functional couplings, establishing it as a promising framework for multimodal representation learning.

item.title: TinySAM 2: Extreme Memory Compression for Efficient Track Anything Model
item.dc:creator: Zhaoyuan Ding
Yijing Yang
Han Shu
Xinghao Chen
item.description: Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM 2) serves as a core foundation model in the field of video segmentation. Building upon the original SAM model, it introduces a memory bank mechanism and demonstrates outstanding performance in tasks such as semi-supervised video object segmentation and tracking anything. However, the complex computational characteristics of SAM 2's multi-stage image encoder and memory module have raised the barrier to the model's deployment in practical applications. To address this issue, we propose TinySAM 2, a lightweight video segmentation model that balances performance and efficiency. First, a memory quality management mechanism is introduced to select and retain high-informative historical frames as the memory. In addition, a joint-spatial-temporal token compression is proposed that reduces the memory storage and computational cost. Specifically, average pooling is employed to first compress redundancy tokens in the spatial domain. In the temporal domain, informative tokens are selected across frames in the memory bank based on token-level similarity measurement. Besides, we take RepViT as the lightweight image encoder, which further reduces the model parameters. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets such as DAVIS and SA-V demonstrate that TinySAM 2 achieves 90% of the performance of SAM 2.1, with only 7% memory tokens and 3% training data. This study effectively alleviates the bottlenecks in parameter count, computational load, and deployment costs associated with SAM 2, providing a resource-efficient solution for the widespread application of video segmentation models on devices. Published: 2026-05-18T08:05:59+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhaoyuan Ding; Yijing Yang; Han Shu; Xinghao Chen

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM 2) serves as a core foundation model in the field of video segmentation. Building upon the original SAM model, it introduces a memory bank mechanism and demonstrates outstanding performance in tasks such as semi-supervised video object segmentation and tracking anything. However, the complex computational characteristics of SAM 2's multi-stage image encoder and memory module have raised the barrier to the model's deployment in practical applications. To address this issue, we propose TinySAM 2, a lightweight video segmentation model that balances performance and efficiency. First, a memory quality management mechanism is introduced to select and retain high-informative historical frames as the memory. In addition, a joint-spatial-temporal token compression is proposed that reduces the memory storage and computational cost. Specifically, average pooling is employed to first compress redundancy tokens in the spatial domain. In the temporal domain, informative tokens are selected across frames in the memory bank based on token-level similarity measurement. Besides, we take RepViT as the lightweight image encoder, which further reduces the model parameters. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets such as DAVIS and SA-V demonstrate that TinySAM 2 achieves 90% of the performance of SAM 2.1, with only 7% memory tokens and 3% training data. This study effectively alleviates the bottlenecks in parameter count, computational load, and deployment costs associated with SAM 2, providing a resource-efficient solution for the widespread application of video segmentation models on devices.

item.title: HyperVision: A Channel-Adaptive Ground-Based Hyperspectral Vision Pre-trained Backbone
item.dc:creator: Guanyiman Fu
Jingtao Li
Zihang Cheng
Zhuanfeng Li
Diqi Chen
Yan Xu
Fengchao Xiong
Jianfeng Lu
Jun Zhou
item.description: While hyperspectral imaging provides rich spatial-spectral information across hundreds of narrow wavelength bands for precise material identification, ground-based hyperspectral pre-trained backbones remain absent, constrained by varying spectral configurations across sensors, the scarcity and inconsistency of labels, and the limited scale and scene diversity of existing datasets. To address these challenges and enable universal perception, we propose HyperVision, the first ground-based hyperspectral pre-trained backbone. First, to handle varying spectral configurations, HyperVision adopts a channel-adaptive dynamic embedding mechanism to map heterogeneous inputs into a unified token space. Second, to address the scarcity and inconsistency of labels, we introduce a multi-source pseudo-labeling method that fuses semantic representations from both spatial structures generated by SAM2 and fine-grained spectral material information extracted by HyperFree. Third, to compensate for limited dataset scale and enrich scene diversity, a cross-modal knowledge distillation mechanism is utilized to transfer rich semantic representations from a pre-trained RGB vision model to our hyperspectral backbone. Pre-trained on a collection of 15k images from 26 diverse ground-based datasets, HyperVision demonstrates exceptional generalization. Requiring only efficient head-only adaptation without adjusting backbone parameters, it achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to task-specific methods across three downstream tasks under varying sensor configurations, yielding up to a 16.3% relative improvement in hyperspectral semantic segmentation $\mathrm{Acc}_{\mathrm{M}}$, a 2.1% relative gain in object tracking AUC, and a 35.5% reduction in salient object detection MAE. The source code and pre-trained model will be publicly available at https://github.com/lronkitty/HyperVision . Published: 2026-05-17T06:50:06+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guanyiman Fu; Jingtao Li; Zihang Cheng; Zhuanfeng Li; Diqi Chen; Yan Xu; Fengchao Xiong; Jianfeng Lu; Jun Zhou

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

While hyperspectral imaging provides rich spatial-spectral information across hundreds of narrow wavelength bands for precise material identification, ground-based hyperspectral pre-trained backbones remain absent, constrained by varying spectral configurations across sensors, the scarcity and inconsistency of labels, and the limited scale and scene diversity of existing datasets. To address these challenges and enable universal perception, we propose HyperVision, the first ground-based hyperspectral pre-trained backbone. First, to handle varying spectral configurations, HyperVision adopts a channel-adaptive dynamic embedding mechanism to map heterogeneous inputs into a unified token space. Second, to address the scarcity and inconsistency of labels, we introduce a multi-source pseudo-labeling method that fuses semantic representations from both spatial structures generated by SAM2 and fine-grained spectral material information extracted by HyperFree. Third, to compensate for limited dataset scale and enrich scene diversity, a cross-modal knowledge distillation mechanism is utilized to transfer rich semantic representations from a pre-trained RGB vision model to our hyperspectral backbone. Pre-trained on a collection of 15k images from 26 diverse ground-based datasets, HyperVision demonstrates exceptional generalization. Requiring only efficient head-only adaptation without adjusting backbone parameters, it achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to task-specific methods across three downstream tasks under varying sensor configurations, yielding up to a 16.3% relative improvement in hyperspectral semantic segmentation $\mathrm{Acc}_{\mathrm{M}}$, a 2.1% relative gain in object tracking AUC, and a 35.5% reduction in salient object detection MAE. The source code and pre-trained model will be publicly available at https://github.com/lronkitty/HyperVision .

item.title: Multi-modal prompt interaction for visual unsupervised domain adaptation
item.dc:creator: Yanan Li
Shiyu Quan
Feiyang Zhang
Hao Lu
Zhijiang Li
Junfeng Qu
item.description: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to develop a model capable of transferring knowledge from a well-labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain. Traditional UDA methods attempt to learn invariant features by aligning data distributions between source and target domains. These methods simply map labels to one-hot encodings, which fail to capture the intrinsic semantic correlations among categories. Large Vision-Language pre-trained Models (VLMs), such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), consider both image and label information, offering new possibilities for UDA tasks. The UDA methods utilize CLIP predominantly concentrate on adjusting text prompts. They neglect certain visual context information that is lost during the encoding phase, which hinders knowledge transfer. To effectively utilize multi-modal information, a novel framework M ulti- m odal P rompts I nteraction ( M2PI ) is proposed. Specifically, the discarded pre-trained knowledge is aggregated into dual-branch learnable prompt tokens, where regularization is imposed on standard prompt learning. The two prompt branches interact via a prompt interaction module. This process is further regularized through an instance-discrimination contrastive loss and an information maximization loss. M2PI achieved the best average classification accuracy of 78.6%/87.6% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 and 63.8% with ViT-B/16 on the Office-Home and DomainNet datasets, and the second-best average classification accuracy of 88.1%/90.8% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 on the VisDA-2017 dataset. The results on three UDA benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of M2PI over the state-of-the-art approaches. Published: 2026-05-20T23:27:28+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.793 (must_read)

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133941

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to develop a model capable of transferring knowledge from a well-labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain. Traditional UDA methods attempt to learn invariant features by aligning data distributions between source and target domains. These methods simply map labels to one-hot encodings, which fail to capture the intrinsic semantic correlations among categories. Large Vision-Language pre-trained Models (VLMs), such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), consider both image and label information, offering new possibilities for UDA tasks. The UDA methods utilize CLIP predominantly concentrate on adjusting text prompts. They neglect certain visual context information that is lost during the encoding phase, which hinders knowledge transfer. To effectively utilize multi-modal information, a novel framework M ulti- m odal P rompts I nteraction ( M2PI ) is proposed. Specifically, the discarded pre-trained knowledge is aggregated into dual-branch learnable prompt tokens, where regularization is imposed on standard prompt learning. The two prompt branches interact via a prompt interaction module. This process is further regularized through an instance-discrimination contrastive loss and an information maximization loss. M2PI achieved the best average classification accuracy of 78.6%/87.6% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 and 63.8% with ViT-B/16 on the Office-Home and DomainNet datasets, and the second-best average classification accuracy of 88.1%/90.8% with ResNet/ViT-B/16 on the VisDA-2017 dataset. The results on three UDA benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of M2PI over the state-of-the-art approaches.

item.title: Knowledge Diffusion-Based Adaptive Alignment with Hierarchical Context for Video Temporal Grounding
item.dc:creator: Ran Ran
Jiwei Wei
Yang Yang
Guoqing Wang
Kuien Liu
Jie Zou
Heng Tao Shen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) aims to localize the temporal moment that corresponds to a natural-language query in an untrimmed video. However, VTG faces two core challenges: narrowing the semantic gap between concise text queries and complex visual content, and extracting discriminative moment cues from long background-dominant sequences. To address these challenges, we propose Knowledge Diffusion Alignment (KDA), a diffusion-based framework that operates in the semantic feature domain. At its core, a Background Residual Diffusion Model (BRDM) starts from adaptive global video knowledge and, under query conditioning, progressively removes query-irrelevant background residuals to synthesize moment knowledge. In addition, to provide a strong conditional prior and multi-scale supervision, Hierarchical Video Knowledge Extraction (HVKE) aggregates context at multiple temporal granularities, producing both video and moment knowledge. KDA is trained in three stages: first learning target-aware moment knowledge, then aligning the diffusion process to that target, and finally performing end-to-end inference using BRDM-generated knowledge. The synthesized moment knowledge is injected into knowledge prompt reasoning, where a Dynamic Moment-aware Encoder (DME) adaptively generates dynamic temporal convolution kernels conditioned on the moment knowledge, coupling efficient local modeling with global self-attention. For prediction, a Spans-Enhanced Hierarchical Decoder (SEHD) constructs a temporal pyramid and adaptively selects the most relevant initial spans to exploit intrinsic alignment cues. Experiments across public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed KDA outperforms the state-of-the-art methods, and extensive ablations further verify the effectiveness of the module design. The code is available at https://github.com/Evangelion09/HKDA. Published: 2026-05-19T19:49:41+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.793 (must_read)
